chunks.text:
desire for murder toward acceptable targets to earn a sort of redemption with shows like Showtime’s Dexter (Cerone et al. 2006) and HBO’s Barry (Berg et al. 2018). In 2000, Oh Brother, Where Art Thou? rebooted Homer’s Greek classic The Odyssey into a 1937 Mississippi setting with a story that follows three redeemed outlaws who escape a chain gang and are nearly killed by the obstinate lawman even after he receives word that they were pardoned (Coen 2000). In 2001, The Last Castle told the story of General Eugene Irwin, who is court-martialed and sent to a maximum-security military prison run by an obstinate lawman who eventually murders him (Lurie 2001). In 2002, Omar Epps starred in Conviction , a ﬁlm based on the life of outlaw Carl Upchurch, who earns his redemption by organizing a nationwide peace summit for US gangs even as the local police act as lawmen who continue to harass and arrest him (Sullivan). In 2005, FOX’s television series Prison Break hit prime time with redeemed outlaw Michael Scoﬁeld, a character who earns his

Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 7 of 15 redemption by breaking his innocent brother out of a corrupt prison (Scheuring). In 2006 it was Let’s go to Prison! , a slapstick comedy that pits redeemed outlaw Nelson Biederman IV against a prison full of carefree archetypal lawmen and unrepentant villains who run the place (Odenkirk 2006). In 2009, Jim Carey and Ewan McGregor played redeemed outlaws in the comedy ﬁlm I Love you Phillip Morris , yet another representation of prison as a space where abuse is common and unpunished, and where escape is the only real option for redemption (Requa). By the 2010s, race, class and gender were shaping cultural attitudes and academic scholarship related to crime and punishment. Michelle Alexander’s (2010) The New Jim Crow was released in 2010, ushering in an era of critical research aimed at understanding and undoing the cultural stigma of incarceration while acknowledging the ways one’s identity informs one’s experience of punishment. Jenji Kohan’s (Kohan 2013–2019) blockbuster Orange is the New Black was released in 2013, introducing redeemed outlaw Piper and a

long list of obstinate lawmen who treat her and everyone else in the prison so poorly that a riot breaks out in season four. Kohan explained the show’s success by pointing to Piper’s whiteness in an interview with NPR’s Terry Gross: In a lot of ways Piper was my Trojan Horse. You’re not going to go into a network and sell a show on really fascinating tales of black women, and Latina women, and old women and criminals. But if you take this white girl, this sort of ﬁsh out of water, and you follow her in, you can then expand your world and tell all of those other stories. (Gross 2013) Throughout these various updates in cinematic crime stories, the archetypal outlaw and lawman have remained stoically unchanged. There is, in every story of crime and punishment, an outlaw who seeks some sort of redemption, and a lawman who will not offer it. The archetypal outlaw and his counterpart, the lawman, have been repackaged and resold as different characters in “new” movies for more than a century. Despite their

chunks.text:
apparent idiosyncrasies, each portrayal continues to display similar characteristics. We know them so well that introductions are not necessary, a boon to writers who are spared the hassle of drawing those lines in for us. Contemporary television shows and ﬁlms that focus on imprisonment have also fostered the evolution of a clich éprison setting. The success of these ﬁlms usually depends on the ability of their creators to emphasize the macabre while minimizing the banal, thereby painting a skewed picture of the prison industrial complex. We do not tune in to prisons on screen to see normal people living boring lives; we turn in to see the monsters in our nightmares do awful things to one another in conﬁned spaces far away from our living rooms. 3. The Archetypal Prison The outlaw and the lawman are in place, but the stage must still be set. In prison cinema, that stage is normally designed to ﬁt one of three archetypes, which I shall refer to as prison-as-penance, prison as a playground, and prison as a paradox. Like the characters

we meet inside these cinematic spaces, the differences which appear to set each production apart as original often disappear under scrutiny. Additionally, like those who occupy them, the prisons we see on screen give us tools for understanding our world. Unfortunately, the restricted nature of actual prison spaces makes it difﬁcult for viewers to compare what they see on screen to real life, leaving us reliant on representations to make sense of our world. Given Hollywood’s propensity to feed us what we demand—violence, mayhem and drama in the case of prison cinema—it is only natural that we come to view prisons as horriﬁc institutional spaces required in any functioning society, and as such, as the only thing standing between us and the monsters we see on TV (Yousman 2009). 3.1. Prison-as-Penance “I completed all the programs. I followed the rules. But I still wound up back in prison.” Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 8 of 15 I heard this story dozens of times during my incarceration, and I continue to hear it

from my incarcerated students today. Given our ongoing crisis with recidivism, it’s easy to see why. The most recent data is in line with US history: 82% of all released prisoners were rearrested within 10 years of their release from prison, and 61% of all released prisoners wound up back in prison between 2008-2018 (Antenangeli and Durose 2021). In the United States, redemption has become the exception, not the rule. But those who ﬁnd themselves stuck in the revolving door of criminal corrections seldom ﬁt the mold of stereotypical monsters dreamed up by Hollywood. The Bureau of Justice Statistics has repeatedly found that nearly half of all incarcerated people never receive a write-up for a rule violation during their entire sentence (Stephen 1989; Steiner and Wooldredge 2008). Incarcerated people frequently ask me to write letters of support to include with their requests for entry into overburdened educational and recovery programs. Prisons are packed with people who have been trying to earn redemption for decades only to be ultimately denied it regardless of what they do.

chunks.text:
The trope of prison-as-penance portrays the penitentiary as a terrible-yet-necessary place where those who do awful things are sent for punishment. If they follow the rules and change their ways, outlaws in penance stories are eventually allowed to leave their past behind, while those who remain imprisoned indeﬁnitely are products of their own poor decisions—bad guys of the worst sort designed to strike terror into the hearts of viewers. The ultimate punishment—death—is the natural endpoint of prison-as-penance narratives. Whether at the hands of state employees or others in prison, death looms over the heads of the incarcerated as a reminder that more punishment is always available if one continues to reject traditional redemption. The lawmen in penance narratives are depicted as good guys who protect the public from those who would otherwise run amok and endanger us all. Redemption in a prison-as-penance story is earned through successful navigation of the prison space, which usually develops alongside a sincere sense of regret for one’s crimes, an obvious change in one’s ways, and, eventually, a devotion to helping others avoid a

similar path in the future. Despite the violent prison environment with traps around every corner, the redeemed outlaw in a prison-as-penance story learns something from their incarceration that leads to a better life after release. Prison-as-penance reﬂects our cultural understanding of outlaws, lawmen and prisons so well that it shows up at some point in nearly all outlaw stories. Michel Foucault explained why in his groundbreaking work Discipline and Punish , where he traces the evolution of laws that were originally designed to exclude rule breakers from the larger social body through shame or death, but are now designed to bring rule-breakers back into the social fold by enticing (disciplining) them into conforming to social and cultural institutions (Foucault 1975). Prison-as-penance exempliﬁes Foucault’s disciplinary apparatus aimed at producing self-regulation through technologies of power: “the expiation that once rained down upon the body must be replaced by a punishment that acts in depth upon the heart, the thoughts, the will, the inclinations” (p. 16). The result is a change of “heart” leading to a new performance of

identity more in line with socially acceptable standards: prison-as-penance. Prison-as-penance is more than just boring cells and squeaky bars. Often what the outlaw experiences in prison cinema goes far beyond what we would normally consider disciplinary, from torture to sexual assault sustained at the hands of other incarcerated people, to outright murder. When the end result of such stories is a change of heart, viewers are shown an image of prison as a successful tool of correction; it might be rough (at times even torturous), but at least it is effective in forcing some bad people to reevaluate their morals. Additionally, in those rare cases when even the worst punishment available fails to change the outlaw’s ways and murder is not an appropriate option, prison-as-penance is always willing to cage the worst bad guys until the day they die, protecting viewers from the characters on screen who might otherwise haunt us in our sleep. InConviction , outlaw Carl Upchurch completes his prison sentence and walks out a changed man, abandoning his prior gang associations and eventually hosting a nationwide

chunks.text:
peace summit in Kansas City (Sullivan 2002). In Dead Man Walking , prison-as-penance Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 9 of 15 plays out as the main character nears his execution date and ﬁnally confesses in the 11th hour, painting prison and its ultimate end game—the death penalty—as effective tools of closure when nothing else works (Robbins 1995). In Short Eyes , penance is delivered not by state ofﬁcials, but by those in jail when a man who is guilty of abusing children is about to be released because of faulty evidence, causing the outlaws-turned-lawmen to step in and enforce punishment (murder) where the system is incapable (Young 1977). InAmerican History X , violent sexual assault at the hands of other incarcerated people initiates an unexpected change of heart in the main character, who then proceeds to ﬁght against Neo-Nazi culture once released (Kaye 1998). In The Green Mile , we meet “Wild Bill” Wharton, a man who celebrates his upcoming execution and even swings from the bars screaming, “he’s frying now!” while his neighbor is in the electric chair (Darabont 1999).

Wharton reminds us that even though bad guys beyond redemption might exist, the prison system always has an answer, up to and including death. When we are shown image after image of prison as a place that keeps us safe from creepy characters who might otherwise do us harm while rehabilitating those who are willing to change, what else can we do but come to the conclusion that prison is a vital component for a functioning society? Without that preexisting belief, such narratives would not make sense (Yousman 2009). Without the constant ﬂow of “new” narratives, such beliefs would not be sustainable. 3.2. Prison as a Playground Prison is many things, but most of them are boring. The ﬁrst sixty days were the worst. Classiﬁed as “maximum security inmates” until the prison administration completed a personalized security-threat evaluation, we were locked up for 23 h each day. We left our cells for chow, for medical callouts, and for nothing else. Books were hard to come by and seldom included both halves. Mail and phone calls

were restricted until we were classiﬁed and housed at a permanent facility. Property was scarce because most of us were new arrivals with nothing but the clothes and bedding handed out by the state when we arrived. Jackson was the absence of stimulation taken to the extreme. Any movement outside what was requested was forbidden, and anyone caught breaking the rules was immediately reassessed to a higher security level. There are no playgrounds in prison. In representations of prison as a playground, the rules that restrict the movements and possessions of those in prison do not apply to a subset of incarcerated characters, often the worst behaved in the facility. Playground depictions showcase incarcerated shot callers who are not only immune to the regulations, but who can navigate the space of prison and use it to achieve their often-wicked ends. Leonidas Cheliotis described the ﬁctional prison setting as a place where: Imagination tends to be taken on a sensational journey into spaces where the false and the ﬁctional arise victorious from the ashes of the real. Prisons are usually

typecast either as dark institutions of perpetual horror and virulent vandalism or idyllic holiday camps offering in-cell television and gourmet cuisine on the back of taxpayers. (Cheliotis 2010, p. 175)

chunks.text:
Playground depictions are on full display when sexual assault appears commonplace and unpunished, when restricted items are easy to get, when ofﬁcers can be bribed or blackmailed with ease, when escapes are carried out without much trouble, or when orchestrated hits are put out against those in prison. Many of the characters in prison as a playground thrive in the prison space, unbothered by the minor restrictions that do exist, and always ready to commit additional crimes should an opportunity arise (it always does). As viewers, we come to accept these depictions as representative of prison (Yousman 2013; Brown 2009). We might believe them to be exaggerated or glamorized, but we also view them as based on truths (De Certeau 1984; Yousman 2009). When the monsters on our screens live in taxpayer-funded facilities where they can continue committing crimes and victimizing others, how else can we respond except with disgust at the idea that prisons simply are not tough enough? When guards regularly ignore their own safety to smuggle Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 10 of 15

in cellphones, drugs or even weapons, what else can we do but desire harsher security and more restrictive rules (Yousman 2013). Representations of prison as a playground are the bread and butter of movie producers. In Shawshank, Red is the guy “who can get you anything” (Darabont 1994). In Goodfellas , Paulie has a stockpile of steak, lobster, cigars and liquor, all served alongside “meatballs made with three kinds of meat: veal, beef and pork” (Scorsese 1990). As he explains, “wise guys” do not really mind being sent to prison: “everyone else in the joint was doing real time, all mixed together living like pigs . . .we owned the joint.” I Love you Phillip Morris is packed with playground scenes, including men masturbating in public spaces and having sex without restrictions, contraband being bought, sold, and handed out as gifts, and even escapes that are so easy to pull off they become the central theme of the ﬁlm (Requa and Ficarra 2009). In Prison Break , the main characters, who eventually break out, manage to

bribe guards, possess restricted items (including weapons), move around the prison freely, dig a hole in the ﬂoor of the ofﬁcer break room, and constantly assault one another at liberty (Scheuring 2005). In Breaking Bad , meth-kingpin “Heisenberg” manages to have ten people who are locked up inside three different prisons killed within the span of just a few minutes (MacLaren 2012). Prison as a playground paints an image of the criminal justice system wherein incarcerated people are seldom contained (or containable), where correctional ofﬁcers have neither the desire nor the ability to prevent them from committing additional crimes, and where anything goes once one is in prison, a characterization that is dialectically opposed to the way most folks feel that prisons ought to be managed. Much of the violence in representations of prison as a playground is sexual in nature, with the victimizers perpetrating their crimes at will and without penalty. There is a long cinematic history of anti-LGBTQ+ sentiment; as hooks explains, “Homophobic construction of gay sexual practice in mass media consistently reinforce the stereotypical notion that

chunks.text:
gay folks are predators, eager to feast upon the innocent” (Hooks 1994, p. 16). It is even worse in prison cinema, where viewers often expect to encounter a special kind of evil. It is so common to see sexual assault in depictions of correctional environments that we are seldom surprised when such storylines play out. Helen Eigenberg and Agnes Baro found that “while most studies on male rape in prison suggest that it is a relatively rare event,” in contemporary movies, “the inclusion of at least a reference to male rape and/or a peripheral rape scene has become a standard part of prison ﬁlm production” (Eigenberg and Baro 2003, pp. 64, 86). Dawn Cecil notes a similar thread running through women’s prison ﬁlms: “These ‘babes-behind-bars’ ﬁlms perpetuate highly sexualized images of female prisoners . . .they do not necessarily seek to educate—instead they aim to titillate” (Cecil 2007, p. 305). In Prison Break , a character known as T-Bag regularly dominates others by turning his pockets inside out and forcing victims to hold them while following him

around the yard, a move that would get you written up in any real prison (Scheuring 2005; Federal Bureau of Prisons 2012). In 2002, soft drink giant 7-Up ran a commercial where a man hands out cans of soda between cell bars until he drops one and quips, “I’m not picking that up,” a shout-out to the trope of sexual victimization around every corner (Walker 2002). In Barbershop , when J.D. is told that someone else is going to prison for his crime, he quips, “don’t drop the soap” (Story 2002). In prison ﬁlms, sex sells, especially if its uninvited. In actual prisons, the rate of sexual assault hovers around 1.3% of incarcerated people per year (Buehler 2021). The spectacle is misleading, to say the least. The extreme storylines offered in prison cinema would not work if the space was not already stocked with the bad guys Hollywood dreams up to turn out stomachs. Sexual assault is commonplace and unpunished in playground ﬁlms, but it only happens because

many of those inside enjoy the sexual victimization of others. Prison riots and violent murders only work when there are plenty of characters to do the assaulting. And those characters are often superhuman in their strength, endurance and cruelty. In Fast & Furious 8, ex-cop Luke Hobbs gives us an image of prison as a playground when he is locked in the same maximum-security facility as his archrival, Shaw (Gray 2017). The action in this playground for hyperviolent men reaches a crescendo when Hobbs and Shaw are both Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 11 of 15 unexpectedly released from their cells and, of course, immediately begin ﬁghting with each other, along with dozens of taser-wielding guards dressed in body armor. Since the trope of prison as a playground relies on evil monsters who are always a threat to social order and nearly impossible to subdue, producers opened all the cell doors along with those of the two main characters, setting their cinematic monsters free. Unsurprisingly, all-out war ensues when everyone comes charging out to immediately attack ofﬁcers and one another,

llm.reason: The chunk is well-focused on the portrayal of sexual assault and prisoner stereotypes in prison films, providing solid examples and contrasts with reality.

chunks.text:
because that is what incarcerated people are like in the movies. A stereotypical Hollywood prisoner is not only hyperviolent, but also hard to contain and incredibly strong, a trope performed by Hobbs when a guard shoots him with a beanbag shotgun and the bullets bounce off his chest without injury. He quips, “beanbag shotgun; big mistake,” then throws one guard across the room and shoots another with his own gun. Prison as a playground tempts viewers to stay tuned in with stories about our worst fears come to life. The redeemed outlaw in a playground ﬁlm is stuck in the box of penance, where they must navigate the penitentiary and survive constant threats to safety and integrity. However, one man’s penance is frequently another man’s playground, and producers have learned well that viewers eat up the drama of unwritten rules, confusing victimization, and counterproductive rehabilitation in prison ﬁlms. Prisons on screen do not contain or reform their residents; they make them more dangerous, antisocial and heartless. Enter the trope of prison as a paradox. 3.3. Prison as a Paradox

“You are Inmate Boyce, 470236? Is that correct?” Parole hearings are the most terrifying part of incarceration for most people, far more threatening than showers, the chow hall, or the prison yard. The three-person panel was tasked with deciding whether I deserved a shot at early release—at redemption—and they were well prepared. Each member had two folders, a thick one packed with my criminal past of arrests for hustling and stealing, and another one, thinner than the ﬁrst, that contained my institutional history. I had just a single ticket over the course of the prior year, and as one member quickly pointed out, I had managed to complete one of the prison’s only educational programs—a typing class. I’d also forwarded them a letter confessing my addiction and expressing my sincere desire to continue my recovery after release. I was only 25 years old, and I’d been locked up for a few of those years, so despite an impressive record of convictions, they’d all come over a relatively short period of time.

The board said a few more words about their concern for community safety and “offender rehabilitation,” then they sent me on my way to make their decision in private. A few weeks later, my release papers showed up; I’d been deemed an acceptable risk. According to the Federal Bureau of Prisons, incarceration should serve the two-fold purpose of threat containment and threat reduction (rehabilitation): . . .to protect society by confining offenders in the controlled environments of prisons and . . .[to] provide work and other self-improvement opportunities to assist offenders in becoming law-abiding citizens. (US Federal Bureau of Prisons 2022) The trope of prison as a paradox ﬂips these stated goals to their opposites, painting prison as a space that expands threats and increases criminality. The end result of paradox narratives is an understanding of prison as a dysfunctional institution that worsens any problems it claims to solve. Prison cinema often plays on all our worst fears and our deepest frustrations with human nature. On-screen prisons are not just cages. Once the script is memorized and the

chunks.text:
cameras are rolling, prisons become magical spaces of terror and spectacle where viewers can travel at will without fear of physical harm or emotional trauma.2In those spaces, we are primed to accept the bizarre and expect the extreme. Aiming to please, bizarre and extreme is exactly what producers and writers feed us, knowing we are unlikely to challenge storylines that would strike us as unbelievable if set anywhere outside of prison. As a result, we often come to understand prison as a paradox: as a place where people who Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 12 of 15 commit crimes are sent for rehabilitation only to wind up more prone to criminality and victimization than before they were arrested. InThe Shawshank Redemption , the warden recruits the outlaw as an illegal accountant, showing an image of prison where crime is not only allowed, but encouraged by those in charge (Darabont 1994). Both Life(Demme 1999) and Cool Hand Luke (Rosenberg 1967) include ﬁght scenes where the main outlaw is beaten by one of the largest men in the prison

in full view of prison ofﬁcials, providing an image of prison as a place where violence is encouraged and ﬁghting is normalized. In Short Eyes , the prison administration fails to prevent incarcerated characters from committing a group murder, showing an image of jail where an arrest for minor crimes can inevitably lead to participation in organized killing (Young 1977). By season three of Oz, Tobias Beecher, who is sent to prison for a drunk driving accident resulting in someone’s death, has been transformed from a peaceful man into a throat-slashing murderer, painting prison as a space that, as Damien Echols has said, can “send a man to prison for writing bad checks and then torment him there until he becomes a violent offender” (Fontana 2003; Echols 2012, p. 14). Within ﬁve minutes of arriving at his ﬁrst prison in Blood In Blood Out , young outlaw Milko is sexually assaulted and forced to join a gang that eventually crafts him into a stereotypical violent recidivist

(Hackford 1993). People do not go to prisons like these to become contributing members of society; they go there to learn new tricks and experience new traumas. The paradoxical nature of such scenes leaves viewers with the impression that those who have been to prison are worse when they get out than they were when they went in. Prison as a paradox reminds us, wrongly, that despite its claims of containing threats and rehabilitating redeemable outlaws, prison often increases the social threat of criminality. Representations of prison as a twisted game where villains enforce confusing rules and victimize others for unclear reasons paint the correctional space as fraught with danger around every corner and incapable of offering effective rehabilitation of any sort. For the victimizers, prison is painted as a playground for sadistic behavior. For their victims, prison is painted as torturous penance. However, for the viewer of such stories, prison is transformed into a paradoxical trap—a place where the incarcerated must learn to victimize others if they are to avoid becoming victims themselves. Any time characters inside a

chunks.text:
cinematic prison ﬂaunt the rules to constantly hurt one another, viewers must contend with the paradox of an expensive prison system that neither contains threats to social order nor rehabilitates offenders, the two claimed goals of criminal corrections. Unlike representations of prison-as-penance or prison as a playground, prison as a paradox opens a space for viewers to reconsider the effectiveness of the criminal justice system. That is why producers usually slide out of paradox representations and back into the more comfortable ground of playground or penance by the conclusion of most stories. 4. Updating the Spectacle We learn from media whether we want to or not. What we learn comes to inform our perspective of the world, and our ability to understand it and explain it to others (Yousman 2009, 2013; Hooks 1994; Dixon 2017; Donovan and Klahm 2015). When the spaces we learn about on screen are restricted from public oversight, the spectacle becomes conﬂated with our understanding of the world. Even in spaces where most of us can easily verify what

we see on television or ﬁlm, we usually do not bother to do so. How often have you looked for the hatch in the roof of an elevator only to realize it does not exist, glanced at an air vent in an ofﬁce and thoughts, “a human couldn’t crawl through that,” or witnessed an actual police standoff and realized they do not yell, “cover me!” and run into the line of ﬁre? Probably more than once, and those are spaces many of us can access on a regular basis. All the worse when it comes to prisons, which most of us never have the opportunity or the desire to enter in person. Our cultural understanding of prison and incarceration is aligned with our cultural demand for updated spectacles and repackaged stories of mayhem and murder. We learn about drugs, crime and punishment from our televisions and movies, and what we learn is Humanities 2023 ,12, 10 13 of 15 often a far cry from reality. Our media reﬂects a culture obsessed with mass incarceration,

which has become a permanent staple of US life. Any change in our cultural understanding of crime and punishment must begin and end with a cultural update in our representations of crime and punishment. Until we come to see those in prison as worthy of redemption and capable of achievement, we will continue to demand the same stereotypical spectacles. And until the spectacles are updated to reﬂect our demand for humanity, we will continue to view those behind bars as occasional outlaws amongst hordes of villains. What, then, can producers do to draw an audience? If mayhem and murder are what sells, how can we expect salespeople to peddle less popular products? The answer is not to do away with the spectacle, but to update it. The revolution has already begun, and it does not require irredeemable ghouls to keep us tuned in. The world of podcasting offers some rich examples. The award-winning Ear Hustle , hosted by people who are (or who have been) incarcerated and recorded inside San Quentin

chunks.text:
Prison, currently has nine seasons available for download. Ear Hustle is devoted to sharing the stories of incarcerated people who are working for change. Episodes feature true-crime narratives from guests, along with the real stories of those in prison: their histories, their accomplishments, and their ongoing attempts are redemption. The University of Denver Prison Arts Initiative has been recording and releasing episodes of its prison-based podcast With(in) since 2019, focusing on the stories and pathways to success of those inside Colorado prisons. The topics might at times be titillating and salacious, but they are also personal and humanized, told by those who lived them and expanded beyond the criminal charges which the world often uses to deﬁne those of us who have been to prison. Such productions feed our desire for the taboo, but they do so in a way that paints those in prison as humans who made mistakes and who are capable of redemption. They are part of a new wave of productions that present what Nellis has called “The newly emergent ‘convict criminology,’

produced in the USA by ex-cons (usually imprisoned as a result of the ‘war on drugs’) who have subsequently become academics” (Nellis 2009, p. 144). New prison narratives like Ear Hustle and With(in) also focus on the various ways the current design of the system makes redemption difﬁcult while sharing stories of those who have overcome the odds against them and worked to change their lives. Of course, many of us manage to avoid the trap of recidivism after we are released. Many of us, myself included, do more than that. We build careers. We earn degrees. We reenter prison as educators, counselors and program directors. And all of it has an air of drama—that Hollywood magic that comes with prison walls and razor wire on screen. We can have it both ways; we can demand productions that humanize those in prison while also enjoying the interesting stories about how they got there, and how they get out. The original novel begins with his parole, not his escape. 2Understandably, many of us cannot travel to the space of prison ﬁlm without (re)experiencing trauma.

Abstr act Abstr act This ar ticle off ers a Kierk egaar dian r eading of The Shawshank Redemption (1994), ar guing that the film functions not as a mir acle narr ative but as a cinematic enactment of existential theology . While existing scholarship fr equently interpr ets Shawshank thr ough biblical typology or F oucauldian notions of incar ceration, f ew consider how the film dr amatiz es Kierk egaar d’s categories of anxiety , despair , and the leap of faith. Shawshank pr esents prison as mor e than a space of punishment—it becomes a r efuge fr om the “ dizziness of fr eedom, ” a structur ed escape fr om the terrifying bur den of possibility . Brooks’ s suicide tragically embodies what Kierk egaar d calls the “ despair of weakness, ” the inability t o exist as a self outside institutional cer tainty . Andy Dufr esne, b y contr ast, cultiv ates an inwar d life that sur vives

confinement, ultimately per forming a postsecular form of faith gr ounded in human action r ather than divine inter vention. His escape tr ansforms ut opia fr om non-place int o lived space, enacting hope against hopelessness. By r eading the film thr ough Kierk egaar d, supplemented b y Soja ’s Thir dspace and Habermas’ postsecular theor y, this ar ticle r eframes Shawshank as a st ory of salv ation without mir acle, redemption without metaphysics, and hope made r eal thr ough inwar d commitment and outwar d action.

chunks.text:
cinematic texts of the late twentieth century .2 Its emphasis on spiritual resistance to injustice has very likely contributed to its enduring position at the top of IMDb’s global rankings . Adapted from Stephen King’s novella “Rita Hayworth and Shawshank Redemption” (1982), the film belongs to a period i n King’s writing defined less by supernatural horror than by examinations of injustice, masculinity, and institutional power. Set in mid -century America, Shawshank occupies the cultural landscape between postwar optimism and the civil -rights era, a moment when the authority of institutions , law enforcement, prisons, and the state , was both idealized and increasingly suspect in the aftermath of the Kennedy assassination, the rumblings of the Vietnam war and political turmoil preceding Watergate . The film’s w orld is therefore historically grounded in a time when the promise of American justice was rhetorically celebrated but structurally fragile. Viewers and scholars alike often interpret the story of Andy Dufresne as a contemporary Christ narrative , which is largely justified : he suffers unjustly,

maintains hope in the face of despair, liberates others, and ultimately rises beyond the walls that contain him.3 Other readings turn to Foucauldian frameworks, interpreting the prison as a disciplinary heterotopia, a spatial inverse of society.4 While critics often read Shawshank through biblical typology or Foucauldian punishment,5 few have examined the film through an explicitly Kierkegaardian lens. Existing scholarship in religion and film tends to foreground the story’s Christ symbolism, but rarely its existential theology, its treatment of inwardness, or its pronouncedly postsec ular understanding of redemption. These existing accounts are compelling and substantial , but they remain largely tethered to a binary of confinement and freedom that the film quietly subverts in many ways . In Shawshank , prison is much more than simply the site of oppression. It is also a refuge from something more fundamental and perhaps more frightening than punishment: existential anxiety. Danish philosopher Søren Kierkegaard defines anxiety as “the dizziness of freedom,” the vertigo that arises when a h uman 1Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption Published by DigitalCommons@UNO, 2026

being confronts possibility .6 By juxtaposing confinement with inner freedom and profundity, Shawshank visualizes this paradox with striking clarity. Viewers fear for Andy , as he encounters violence, corruption, solitary confinement , even rape. Yet the prisoners themselves fear something else more than anything : life beyond the walls. As the film’s narrator Red reflects, “first you hate these walls… then you depend on them.” The prison eliminates choice, eliminates uncertainty, and conseque ntly eliminates anxiety. It is a refuge from the burden of being a self (within a society) . An inmate, released after fifty years of incarceration, encounters the terror of freedom and chooses death rather than possibility. His tragedy confirms Kierkegaard ’s claim that despair is not dramatic suffering, but a failure to become oneself.7 In stark contrast to this case, who epitomizes resignation in his suicide, Andy Dufresne becomes a figure of what Kierkegaard calls the “knight of faith ,”8 albeit in thoro ughly secular fashion. He does not possess

chunks.text:
certainty but acts without it. This is underscored by h is patient building of the prison library, his teaching of Tommy, and his decades -long escape plan that all enact a hope that has no rational guarantee —partic ularly in light of the fate that has befallen him . Andy ’s humaneness is portrayed in the many ways he bruises and bleeds like any man, yet transforms the world around him. In Charles Taylor’s terms, Andy lives entirely within the secular “immanent frame,”9 yet generates a form of redemption that Radical Theology describes as Christianity without metaphysical intervention: salvation as human action.10 Crucial to the elevated humanist depiction of Andy, Red becomes his evangelist : particularly the film’s fi nal voiceover is nothing less than the gospel according to Red , as I will attempt to show . This article argues that The Shawshank Redemption is perhaps best understood not as a miracle narrative, but as a cinematic enactment of Kierkegaard’s existentia l theology. Prison suppresses

anxiety by suppressing possibility. True liberation demands the leap of faith. The beach in Mexico , the film’s utopia , begins as a non -place and becomes real only through human belief and action. The movie insists that anxiety is imprinted on the human condition, but so is hope, and neither should be 2Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 DOI: 10.32873/uno.dc.jrf.30.01.44 suppressed –as Kierkegaard first explained almost two centuries ago . To “get busy living” is thus to embrace both. Existing theological readings that foreground Shawshank ’s Christ -figure dynamics are persuasive, and nothing in the present argument requires their dismissal. Indeed, the film repeatedly borrows Christian grammar (suffering, witness, conversion, exodus, and rebirth ) while staging institutional religion (above all, the wa rden’s) as compromised and coercive. The claim advanced here is narrower: whatever biblical typology the film invites, its redemptive causality remains strikingly immanent. Shawshank ’s “salvation” is not delivered as miracle or metaphysical interruption, b ut

enacted as inward discipline, patient labor, and the risky decision to live toward an unguaranteed future. Human Oscillations: Hope, Despair, Anxiety Kierkegaard draws a crucial distinction between fear and anxiety. Fear always has an object: it is directed toward something recognizable in the world. A person fears violence, punishment, humiliation, or death. Anxiety, by contrast, has no object. It arises not from what is, but from all that might be. “Anxiety is the dizziness of freedom,” Kierkegaard writes, “the individual’s openness to possibility.”11 Anxiety appears when the self confronts the realization that it alone must choose its life, and that there is no external guarantee that these choices are secure, meaningful, or right —suggesting an exi stential conundrum that verges on the pathological in modern societies. Rightly so, viewers of The Shawshank Redemption experience fear for Andy : toward the Sisters, the guards, the warden, the threat of solitary confinement. They themselves may also fear the idea of getting caught in the confines of punitive caprice. But the prisoners themselves live in anxiety: the vertigo of possibility

chunks.text:
that returns the moment they imagine freedom. Of importance to the film’s universalist message, Kierkegaa rd himself describes anxiety not as a pathological disturbance but a structural feature of human existence. Where Foucauldian readings rely on the recognition that a prison narrative points the audience towards their own confined state in the wider rule -work of society, a Kierkegaardian perspective on the film shows us anxiety in its most condensed form. In The Concept of Anxiety , he 3Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption inverts the traditional doctrine of original sin. Anxiety does not follow the Fall , but rather it precedes and causes it: Adam experiences freedom first, and that freedom generates the dizziness that makes transgression possible.12 “Anxiety is the presupposition of hereditary sin,” Kierkegaard writes : humankind inherits not only Adam’s guilt, but Adam’s condition.13 Accordingly , anxiety is not a punishment but the deepest expression of what it means to be human: the burden of possibility. Anxiety

and despair are therefore inseparable. In The Sickness Unto Death , Kierkegaard goes on to define despair not as punctuated dramatic suf fering, but as a failure of the self to relate properly to itself.14 A person despairs when they refuse the burden of becoming who they are, when they collapse in the face of possibility and shun the call towards authentic individuation . To exist as a human being is always to stand between actuality and potentiality, never fully complete, always responsible for choosing oneself. Despair is the refusal of this responsibility. It is easier, even comforting, to surrender identity to external structures. Prison, as Shawshank demonstrates, offers precisely this comfort: a life without choice, without risk, without inwardness. Interestingly, this predicament finds expression not only in the pris on’s inmates, further emphasizing the universality of Kierkegaard’s concept. To describe the ways in which individuals relate to this burden, Kierkegaard distinguishes between three modes of existence: the aesthetic, the ethical, and the religious.15 The aesthetic life

avoids responsibility through distraction, pleasure, or routine , any way of escaping the need to choose oneself. The ethical life accepts duty and social identity, but still grounds meaning in external norms and recognition. Only the re ligious life embraces true inward freedom: the acceptance that no objective assurance exists, that truth requires choice, and that one must commit without proof. Faith is therefore not certainty, but risk. “Without risk,” Kierkegaard writes indeed , “no fai th.”16 This inward movement culminates in what Kierkegaard calls the leap of faith. The leap is not irrational, but it is rather trans - rational. It is the moment when the self chooses a path without the security of guarantees, acting “on the strength of th e absurd” —the strength of something that cannot be justified by logic or evidence, but must be affirmed inwardly.17 In Shawshank , this philosophical structure quickly becomes the 4Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 emotional and narrative architecture of the film. Brooks will later exemplify despair in the face of

possibility; Red will live the ethical life until he abandons a life of performed remorse; and Andy will enact the religious life, committing to hope without proof and transforming the world around him through that commitment. Kierk egaard therefore provides not only a theory of human psychology, but a grammar for reading the film: fear belongs to the body, anxiety to the spirit; despair is not the loss of freedom, but the inability to bear it; and redemption is not the elimination of anxiety, but the courage to live with it.

Incarceration as Existential Refuge If anxiety is the dizziness of freedom, then the simplest way to silence anxiety would seemingly be to eliminate freedom. The Shawshank Redemption reveals the paradox of incarceration: punishment can become sanctuary. As Red explains, “first you hate these walls… then you get used to them… then you depend on them.” In Kierkegaardian terms, the prison offers relief from the burden of possibility. Before Andy, there are no choices to make, no futures to construct, no selves to become. The architecture of the institution replaces inward existence with outward routine: where to walk, when to eat, how to speak, when to sleep. Deleuze and Guattari describe such institutional ordering as an example of striated space: regimented, partitioned, and governed by rules that determine movement and inhabitance .18 Shawshank is emblematic of this striation, as every corridor, schedule, and gesture is tightly structure d by authority. The chessboard motif makes this visual : Andy is introduced as someone who “likes chess,” a clue that he understands how striated systems work and how they can

be navigated. Yet his quiet acts of resistance —building the library, teaching lit eracy, playing Mozart, and chiseling the wall —create small incursions of what Deleuze and Guattari call smooth space: zones of improvisation, creativity, and self -determined movement that are juxtaposed with, but also related to striated space . The tunnel is the film’s ultimate line of flight, detouring through the prison’s material grid. The final sho t then completes this spatial logic. Andy and Red stand at the Pacific , what the two 5Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption French philosophers would indeed describe as the archetype of smooth space: open, unbounded, without any walls or coordinates. Notice that Red follows Andy more on the pro position of escaping the narrow heterotopia than cartographic description. Shawshank begins in total striation and ends in pure possibility. The escape is not only physical but assumes an ontological level as well : the self moves from structure to freedom, from confinement to the open. Beforehand, Shawshank epitomizes how s tructure swallows freedom, and with freedom, the

terror of possibility. Kierkegaard calls this form of surrender the despair of weakness : the refusal of the self to stand before its ow n potentiality.19 To despair in this way is not necessarily to suffer spectacularly, but more tragically to evade the responsibility of existence. The men do not merely suffer physical incarceration and lose their liberty , but they lose the very requirement to choose it. The prison makes life easier by eliminating the self. It is not the withdrawal of punishment that terrifies the inmates, but the return of agency. Red describes how the architecture of Shawshank exerts a psychological transformati on: initial resistance gradually gives way to acceptance, until the prisoners depend on the very structures that confine them. Routine has thus become protection. The film makes this idea spatially explicit. The prison functions as what Michel Foucault cal ls a heterotopia , a counter - site that mirrors the structure of society while inverting its values.20 The world outside is chaotic, fast,

chunks.text:
indifferent; the world inside is ordered, predictable, legible. The prison is therefore a refuge from uncertainty more than a locale of spatial confinement . The fact that Shawshank can feel psychologically safer than the outside world is the clearest indication of despair. For Kierkegaard, this would not be the loss of the self, but the refusal of the self. To Red in h is secular environment, it is being “institutionalized.” Jürgen Habermas describes late modernity as “postsecular”: a cultural moment in which belief and unbelief coexist, and where religious vocabulary resurfaces not as doctrine, but as a language for exp eriences that secular rationality cannot dissolve.21 The nomenclature and disciplinary demarcation changes, but not the human experience it tries to analyze. In line with this postsecular environment that film and audience 6Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 share, it is a world confined to what Charles Taylor calls the immanent frame,22 in which anxiety becomes a pervasive condition —possibility without transcendence, freedom without eschatology.

Clinical psychology generally notes that sustained anxiety, when unmanageable, collapses into depression as a shutting down of agency, hope, and inward movement. Shawshank visualizes this postsecular condition with stark clarity. Brooks does not fear death as much as he fears freedom. His despair is psychological more than theological , taking the shape of a paralysis of the self unable to endure possibility and the temporal oscillations it causes in a progressively turbocapitalist world . Andy, by contrast, enacts a se cularized form of faith : hope without metaphysics, and redemption without miracle , brought about by confidence in one’s values and resourcefulness . Habermas’ postsecular describes precisely this landscape: a world stripped of transcendence in which spiritu al needs persist, and where redemption is performed and not bestowed upon the individual . The film also very effectively exposes the emotional logic that makes this refuge so seductive. Freedom requires selfhood, and selfhood requires anxiety. The moment B rooks steps outside the gates, the world becomes

unbearable: too bright, too fast, too open. The prison has not rehabilitated him (whatever that means, as Red will later point out) ; it has protected him from the dizziness of freedom. The same walls that once oppressed him now give him shape, structure, and meaning. Their removal leaves him weightless, without internal form, without a self to return to. Shawshank therefore complicates conventional narratives of liberation. In most prison films, escape marks triumph. Here, escape marks crisis. What the men fear is not confinement, but possibility. If the prison is a cage, it is one that many of its occupants cling to because it kills anxiety. And in killing anxiety, it also kills hope. While the prison init ially appears as a purely material space with the camera focusing on high walls, barred cells, and regimented movement , it quickly reveals itself as something more complex. As Anish Mundra argues , Shawshank cannot be understood simply as a real prison (Soja’s Firstspace) nor merely as the symbolic or imagined space that exists in the minds of prisoners and authorities

(Secondspace).23 Instead, it functions as a Thirdspace, a lived site where materi al structures and 7Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption psychological interpretations collide, contradict, and reshape one another. Shawshank is orderly yet corrupt, punitive yet communal, oppressive yet capable of producing solidarity, learning, and even joy. Importantly, i t is both a real in stitution and a metaphorical world. This is why the same yard is, to Red, a marketplace; to Andy, a quarry for the crumbled wall; and to the authorities, a site of surveillance. The result is what Soja calls a “transcending composite of all spaces ,”24 wher e power, imagination, and embodiment interact. This concept helps illuminate why prison comforts the men as much as it cages them. The Thirdspace produces a “structured refuge” that provides predictable routine, stable identity, and a vanishing of existent ial uncertainty. Freedom would collapse this space and force the self back into anxiety. Brooks’s tragedy, therefore, is not simply institutionalization but the destruction

of the Thirdspace that once gave his life structure. Andy, however, challenges this spatial order. He creates an inner Thirdspace , an interior world built on books, music, imagination, and hope. His inner life safeguards him against both the physical walls (Firstspace) and the oppressive mental logic of imprisonment (Secondspace). When h e plays Mozart over the loudspeakers, he does exactly what Soja describes: he “compromises the space of order,” creating a simultaneous zone of resistance, possibility, and memory . “It was in here,” he explains , pointing to his head , and suggesting unfatho med profundities of inner life.

The Despair of Weakness Brooks Hatlen is the film’s most devastating illustration of what Kierkegaard calls the despair of weakness : the inability to become oneself when confronted with the possibility pointed out above . In The Sickness Unto Death , Kierkegaard writes that despair is not dramatic or spectacular , but quiet and ordinary, the “refusal to be a self.”25 It occurs when the individual cannot bear the weight of freedom and collapses into nonbeing. Brooks’s release from prison is not a victory —it is a death sentence , as he himself senses when pondering the desperate me asure of harming an inmate . The world outside the walls offers him what prison had removed: the dizzying openness of possibility , amplified 8Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 by the disruptive introduction of cars and consumer -oriented infrastructure . Freedom is not relief but terror at its most frightening, as both Kierkegaard and the Shawshank agree . The film’s visual grammar makes this clear.

Inside Shawshank, Brooks is a librarian, a mentor, and a gentle authority. Notably, this role extends across the various spaces discussed in pre vious sections, as Brooks has a role to serve in the official order of inmates, as well as the small acts of deviation that continue to occur, as he smuggles outside goods along with his books. He belongs to a social and spatial order. The prison gives him identity, routine, community, even affection. After his release, the world becomes intolerably bright and fast. “I saw an automobile once when I was a boy, but now they’re everywhere,” he writes. The letters tremble , and he cannot cross a street without f ear. He cannot sleep without the walls that once confined him. His life has become possibility without structure, freedom without anchoring, anxiety with no refuge. Without a place to be, he does not know how to be —as also for the audience, by extension. In Kierkegaard ian terms, Brooks’s inward self has been replaced by the outward routines

of prison life. The institution carried his identity for him. He never had to choose himself, hope for himself, or become himself. He inhabited Soja ’s Thirdspace : not merely the physical prison (Firstspace) nor merely the imagined one (Secondspace), but a lived environment in which the walls provided orientation, stability, and meaning. Shawshank may have physically confin ed Brooks , but it also furnished him with a worl d. His new freedom destroys that world. This is why Brooks’s suicide note is not bitter, but tragically apologetic. “I’ve decided not to stay. I doubt they’ll kick up any fuss, not for an old crook like me.” He is not angry, resentful, or vengeful ––he also hasn’t relapsed into crime. He is empty. A clinical description of the causative trajectory into depression can be the collapse that follows unmanageably sustained anxiety when the nervous system can no longer bear possibility : it shuts down all agency an d desire.26 Brooks’s death appears as an act of despairing passion as well

as a quiet surrender. He dies because he cannot inhabit the world as a self. 9Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption Crucially for our reading , the film makes clear that Brooks’s tragedy is not failure but human vulnera bility. As the audience may identify, i n a fully secular world, there is no metaphysical rescue: no miracle, no providence, no transcendent meaning that steps in to catch him. This is Habermas’s postsecular condition in its starkest form. The prison eliminated anxiety for an entire five decades, and the world immediately reactivated it. Without inward resources (imagination, hope, spiritual or secular identity ) freedom becomes uninhabitable. Brooks , as this section has pointed out, is not crushed by p unishment, but by possibility . His final words (“Maybe I should kill myself. I’m tired of being afraid all the time” ) collapse Kierkegaard’s distinction between fear and anxiety. Fear has objects. Anxiety does not. His last act, carving “Brooks was here,” attempts to give the world a

trace of his existence, a permanent mark that his self could not sustain. It is the aesthetic life’s final gesture: the memorialization of a self that just could not continue. The Shawshank Redemption places no judgment on him. Instead, it uses his death to illuminate what Andy will later survive: the need to create an inward life strong enough to withstand freedom. Brooks dies because he cannot hold himself together when the world demands that he be a self. His death is the fil m’s most heartbreaking conclusion: punishment did not destroy him , but freedom did. In Kierkegaardian terms, Andy embodies the religious mode of existence : the individual who acts without certainty, sustains hope without proof, and commits to a future that has absolutely no guarantee. Andy’s faith is no t grounded in the metaphysical assurance

that the warden carries with him as performative act but derives from an inexhaustible inward discipline . His refusal to surrender imagination, memory, and possibility transforms his imprisonment into a space of mea ning, as the confinement in the hole after the music scene underlines powerfully . He teaches Tommy to read, builds the library letter by letter, and plays Mozart over the loudspeakers , 10Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 not to escape the world, but to re -enchant it (successfully so, as the homogenized bafflement of all who listen informs the viewer) . “That’s the beauty of music,” he tells Red. “They can’t take that away from you.” The Thirdspace he constructs inside his mind is of such stability that it breaks open the prison’s spatial order . Returning to Charles Taylor’s vocabulary, Andy is a fully secular saint: he lives entirely within the immanent frame , a world without divine intervention, miracles, or providence , yet generates a form

of redemption that is recognizably spiritual.27 Radical Theology describes this as Christianity emptied of metaphysical claims but alive as human action.28 Andy is never depicted praying for rescue , but he creates it himself . He does not preach hope; he enacts it. His faith is always lived, not spoken. A subtle but revealing detail reinforces this logic of inward redemption. Andy hides his rock hammer inside a hollowed -out Bible , an object the warden clutches as a symbol of religious authority but never reads. The warden’s faith is theatrical and punitive: he uses Scripture to justify violence, corruption, and exploitation. Andy, by contrast, practices a postsecular Christianity grounded not in metaphysical belief but in action. The Bible does not save him because it is holy : it literally saves him because it conceals the tool of his freedom. Salvation lies within, both literally and figuratively. When, after Andy’s escape, the warden opens the Bible and discovers the hammer inside, he finds a bookmark at

the beginning of Exodus , a book defined by liberation from captivity. More than anything, t he scene exposes the difference between possessing a text and being transformed by it. The warden knows Scripture by memory but not by meaning . Andy , conversely, enacts meaning without ever preaching it. His freedom is the product of inward life, imagination, patience, and hope , ironically the very qualities the prison was designed to extinguish. The warden thus believes in Scripture as text , but Andy believes in Scripture as act. One impriso ns in God’s name , the other escapes by living what the text means. Andy’s breakthrough moment arrives when he begins carving his name into the cell wall —a gesture meant to mark resignation, “Andy was here,” like Brooks or Red. But the wall crumbles. chunks.text:
those who believe it to be. Like Abraham acting “on th e strength of the absurd,” the most powerful leap of faith Kierkegaard focuses on, Andy commits decades of patient labor without knowing the outcome.29 His tunnel, like faith itself, is an act of risk without certainty. The storm that accompanies his escap e is a visual inversion of the Romantic sublime. In Frankenstein , meteorological events herald the moment of Victor’s transgressive creation and subsequent destruction .30 In Shawshank , in contrast, lightning marks Andy’s liberation. The storm provides the cover he needs to enter the sewage pipe, to “swim through a river of shit,” as Red vividly puts it, and emerge clean. The scene indeed evokes baptism, but stripped of metaphysics —as water does not cleanse his soul , but removes the filth of institutional corruption. This is a rare moment of the sacramental stripped of the metaphysical in cinematic history . When Andy bursts from the pipe and stands in the rain with his arms outstretched,

the image resembles a cruciform po sture, but the film refuses miracle. His rebirth is the result of work, patience, and imagination , faith translated into action. His redemption is self-made, not given. Andy’s escape therefore reveals the true meaning of hope in Shawshank as beyond optimis m and fantasy, but as the courage to act without assurance. In a world where despair is easy and the burden of selfhood is heavy, Andy survives by creating an inner life that no walls can contain. He becomes, in Kierkegaard’s sense, a knight of faith in a secular world. At the same time, the film codes Andy’s hope as more than self -belief or mere optimism. Shawshank gives him a recognizably religious literacy (his ease with Scripture in the warden’s cell; the Bible as a recurring object of interpretive s truggle) and frames hope as something with a kind of objective durability —“good things… never die” —rather than a private preference. One can therefore concede a subtle metaphysical horizon in Andy: a sense that reality is not finally chaotic, that there is

an order worth aligning oneself with, and that the human person is answerable to more than expediency. The crucial point, however, is that the film refuses to stabilize this horizon as doctrine , precisely in 12Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 line with its postsecular temper. Shawshank neither evacuates religion nor endorses it in confessional form . Instead, it retains religious grammar as a live register of meaning while relocating soteriology into the immanent. The result is a “porous” transcendence: the language of ultimacy persists, but it is no longer anchored to ecclesial authority or metaphysical guarantee and must be tested and enacted as practice. Andy’s “higher power,” if we may call it that, remains narratively non -specified and pragmatically translated into practices: learning, music, solidarity, and the long work of preparing a future that has no guarantee. Transcendence is not removed, but rendered porous and ambient, while redemption remains something that must be enacted within the world rather than bestowed upon it .

chunks.text:
The Gospel According to Red Andy is Shawshank’s knight of faith, and Red is its evangelist. His arc poetically embodies Kierkegaard’s transformation from the ethical life as one defined by social expectations and external validation to the religious life, which requires inward commitment without certainty. For most of the film, Red is the rationalist, the skeptic, the man who knows how the world works. “Hope is a dangerous thing, ” he warns Andy. “Hope can drive a man insane.” Hope, to Red, is a fantasy, a delusion, an invitation to despair. Better to be realistic, keep one’s head down, and play by the rules. This is the ethical self: the self as defined by roles and institutions , which Red identifies with more than any other inmates we meet . His parole hearings make this visible to the audience . In each early hearing, Red performs rehabilitation. He uses the vocabulary the system wants: alluding to remorse, repentance, reformation. The audience also learns that this is a performative act, a guessing -game that rightly

recognizes the arbitrary interpretation of rehabilitation. Red here does not speak truth but strategy. And every time, the board stamps DENIED. Kierkegaard calls this “the despair of defiance”: the self that maintains control by refusing inwardness, living according to external expectations rather than inward truth.31 Red lives ethically, which is a start, but not authentically. 13Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption Brooks ’s death becomes Red’s turning point. He recognizes that Brooks did everything the institution asked —served his time, obeyed the rules, waited patiently —and yet freedom destroyed him. Where Brooks proves that obedience and routine are not enough, Andy prov es that imagination and inward life can survive even Shawshank’s brutalities. When Red discovers the tin box in the stone wall containing Andy’s letter and the money needed to reach Mexico, he confronts a choice that has no guarantee: violate parole and ri sk imprisonment, or act on faith in a future he cannot see. Even before

that, Red anticipates the leap of faith as h is final parole hearing marks the moment of inward truth. Red no longer performs repentance and refuses the ritual : I look back on the way I was. A young, stupid kid who committed a terrible crime. I want to talk to him. I want to try to talk some sense to him, tell him how things are. But I can’t. That kid’s long gone, and this old man is all that’s left. I look back on him and I want to shak e my head in pity. This is Kierkegaard’s movement from external to internal, from identity as role to identity as self. In a Kierkegaardian sense, Red finally collapses performance and speaks from inwardness. The board grants him freedom the moment he sto ps trying to earn it. This is not providence; it is irony. The system recognizes authenticity only when Red abandons the system’s language. His freedom, like Andy’s, arrives through an inward leap, not outward obedience. Red’s narration over the film’s fin al

moments turns the story into testimony. Andy never preaches, but Red preaches Andy. “I hope… I hope I can make it across the border. I hope the Pacific is as blue as it has been in my dreams. I hope to see my friend and shake his hand. I hope.” The fina l word of the film is not freedom, nor justice, nor redemption. It is hope , spoken as prayer, creed, and confession. This is why I argue the film’s voiceover functions like scripture. Red’s words record the transformation that viewers witness: despair turned to possibility, blank realism to faith, and overwhelming fear to tender hope. Shawshank may offer no divine intervention, but it offers what Habermas calls the postsecular restoration of religious function: consolation, meaning, community, and redemption enacted in a secular world.32 Red becomes the film’s evangelist not because he believes in God, which is never once mentioned, but because he believes in Andy . And therefore, for the first time, in himself. When Red breaks parole and

14Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 boards the bus to Fort Hancock, he performs his leap. He has no assurance that Andy will be there, no promise of safety, no certainty the dream will survive. All was foretold by a seemingly delirious and desperate Andy. But he chooses it anyway. His last line , “I hop e,” is the textual marker of his profound transformation. As every gospel ends with the promise of something more, Shawshank ends with hope as the act that makes the future real. Conclusion: Leaping Out of Heterotopi a’s Confinement For most of the film, Shawshank functions as Foucault’s heterotopia , as a space set apart from society, governed by its own logic, its own rituals, and its own forms of temporal experience. It is a refuge from possibility as much as a site of punishment. Within its walls, the men are protected from the dizzying openness of freedom; the heterotopia gives them structure where the world offers none.

Brooks dies because he cannot survive outside it. Red fears the same fate. For both, the prison is paradoxically livable precise ly because it dissolves the need to choose oneself. Andy alone pushes beyond this heterotopic frame. For him, Shawshank becomes not only Firstspace (material) nor merely Secondspace (imagined), but a Thirdspace : a lived environment where inward possibility reshapes material reality. His inner life , assuming external representation through music, memory, learning, and the secret tunnel , compromises what the institution claims is solid and total. In Kierkegaard’s vocabulary, Andy refuses despair. He refuses t o let the world define the limits of the self. Where Brooks sees only walls, Andy sees another kind of deviation : an opening hidden within the structure. The leap of faith begins long before the escape ; the prison breaks for Andy the moment the wall does. This is why Zihuatanejo matters , even before it materializes . It begins as utopia in the literal sense: no place , an imagined future with no basis in the present. Red justifiably dismisses it as

chunks.text:
fantasy. But for Kierkegaard, possibility becomes real the moment the individual commits inwardly to it. Acting “on the strength of the absurd,” Andy behaves as if Zihuatanejo already exists —saving money, hiding documents, and building rout es. For once, u topia ceases to be dream and becomes task. 15Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption Shawshank enacts what Ernst Bloch calls concrete utopia : not escape from the world, but transformation of it.33 Andy wills the future into being. When Andy stands on the beach and Red walks towar d him, the film reveals the full resonance of that leap , as the heterotopia finally dissolves. The imagined space has become a lived space for him . Zihuatanejo is no longer mere metaphor but geography. Notice that h ope did not rescue Andy; Andy rescued hop e. As Kierkegaard insists, faith is not necessarily believing without proof , it is acting without guarantee . This is what theologian Jürgen Moltmann , drawing on Paul’s idea of hoping

“against hope” (Romans 4:18) , calls a transformative hope as not a passiv e wish, but a force that remakes reality. In more religious terms than the movie makes explicit, hope does not wait for the world to change but acts in the world to change it. Andy’s labor, patience, and imagination enact this theology in material form .34 Zihuatanejo exists because he opens the confines of his world. Andy also escapes not because the world opened for him, but because he opened the world. The journey from Shawshank to the Pacific is therefore a movement from protected despair to dangerous f reedom, and from the denial of possibility to its embrace. Brooks tragically could not inhabit that freedom. Red learns to. Andy shows that utopia, like faith, is not given , it is made. This is the epitome of Kierkegaard’s relation to one’s faith. Shaws hank therefore becomes a postsecular Thirdspace: materially restrictive, psychologically suffocating, yet shot through with invisible apertures of possibility . Andy survives by cultivating an

inner world and only when he discovers that deviation is structu rally possible, that the wall crumbles, does the imagined space become a literal escape route. His freedom is made. In a place built to eliminate possibility, he reveals that possibility was always there , a discovery the film discourages for the majority o f its runtime . Andy’s survival is mental, imaginative, and inward. Every human being, whether free or imprisoned, must spend a lifetime inside their own head. Shawshank suggests that the true prison is not the Firstspace of stone walls, nor the Secondspace of routine and institutional identity, but the collapse of the inner world , as Andy endures because his imagination remains unconfined. His 16Journal of Religion & Film, Vol. 30 [2026], Iss. 1, Art. 44 Thirdspace of hope, memory, and possibility precedes and makes possible his escape. This is why Brooks cannot survive and Andy can. Brooks’s world ends the moment the heterotopia dissolves ––or is replaced by even more opaque counterparts on the other side of the wall . Without structure, his

chunks.text:
inward life collapses into anxiety and then depression. He is destroyed no t by punishment, but by freedom. Red stands between them and begins as a realist, living ethically, performing the role the system expects, suppressing hope because it threatens the fragile equilibrium that keeps him alive. Only when he abandons performanc e, speaks inward truth, and makes his own leap does the world become inhabitable. His final “I hope” becomes confession, creed, and prayer —all without requiring metaphysics. Kierkegaard reminds us that anxiety belongs to the human condition. It is not a pa thology but a structural feature of freedom. Despair is the refusal of that condition; faith is the courage to live within it. The Shawshank Redemption visualizes this philosophy with unusual clarity. Prison removes anxiety by removing possibility, but it also removes selfhood. Freedom demands risk, and the risk is real harm, real failure, real loss. Hope is therefore not naïve , the movie shows us ; it is the only response

that makes freedom livable. In a world without obvious miracles, salvation must be ena cted, not received. For Habermas, the postsecular is the recognition that secular societies have not outgrown spiritual needs. Shawshank affirms this , too. It is not a Christian film, yet it performs redemption. Andy enacts Exodus , whereas Red receives a kind of gospel. The film offers consolation and transcendence while never transcending the world. Its miracles are human: patience, imagination, loyalty, conviction , education . In the end, Shawshank crucially proposes that hope is not mere optimism but work , an inward posture that becomes outward transformation. Escapist utopia will always crumble and emphasize its etymological conundrum , as it is “nowhere” —but a world remade through human action is as close as can come . Andy shows that despair is not in evitable, that possibility remains even where structures appear total, and that the self can be chosen again and again. Remember t hat the last image 17Wilken: Leaping Out of Confinement with Kierkegaard in The Shawshank Redemption

underscores this postsecular notion, as it consists of two men on a beach —no angels, no revelations, no div ine verdict —only freedom, friendship, and hope. As Red says: “I hope.”

Shawshank_Video_Essay

THE SHAWSHANKREDEMPTION

T ue, 11/18/2014 - 11:30Samuel GoldwynTheatre 8949 Wilshire BoulevardBeverly Hills, CA 90211SHAREIn person: Frank Darabont,Morgan Freeman and Tim Robbins.Moderated by Max Brooks.20th Anniversary ScreeningScreenwriter Frank Darabont madehis feature directing debut with anOscar-nominated adaptation ofStephen King’s novella Rita Hayworthand Shawshank Redemption, and over thelast twenty years, this gripping story ofhope and courage has become one ofthe most beloved ﬁlms of its time.Tim Robbins stars as Andy Dufresne,a young banker falsely convicted of themurder of his wife and her lover, whofaces unthinkable brutality as an inmateof Shawshank State Prison.Andy’s accounting skills andhis friendship with a more experiencedprisoner, Ellis Boyd 'Red' Reddingplayed by Morgan Freeman help keephim alive in the hostile setting.However, an unforgivable betrayal leadshim to plot an impossible escape. Forhis directing debut, Darabontassembled a top-notch supporting castwith a memorable performance by thegreat James Whitmore as a longtimeconvict. Darabont was also aided byRoger Deakins’ starkcinematography , Richard Francis-Bruce’s tense editing and ThomasNewman’s emotional score, which allearned Oscar nominations. 1994, 143 minutes, color, 35mm | W ritten and directedby Frank Darabont; based on the novella RitaHayworth and Shawshank Redemption by StephenKing; with Tim Robbins, Morgan Freeman, BobGunton, William Sadler, Clancy Brown, Gil Bellows,Mark Rolston, James Whitmore.

chunks.text:
A modern American classic celebrated its 20th anniversary in high style at the Academy onNovember 18, 2014, when cast and crew members reunited at a special screening of TheShawshank Redemption hosted by W orld W ar Z author Max Brooks. Nominated for sevenAcademy Awards, the ﬁlm was released in the fall of 1994 and marked the feature directorialdebut of its screenwriter, Frank Darabont. The ﬁlm was based on a Stephen Kingnovella entitled Rita Hayworth and Shawshank Redemption, originally published in 1982 in thecollection Diﬀerent Seasons. (Read Stephen King’s thoughts on the ﬁlm here.)Darabont’s ﬁrst eﬀort behind the camera also was a King adaptation, a 30-minute ﬁlm basedon the short story “The W oman in the Room”; it was made with the help of the novelist’s“dollar baby” policy of granting permission to select ﬁlmmakers and students to adapt one ofhis stories for one dollar. Darabont went on to write and direct two more features fromKing’s body of work, The Green Mile (1999) and The Mist (2007), and called the novelist “apatron saint to my career.”On stage at the sold-out Samuel Goldwyn Theater (with the enthusiastic audience givingtwo standing ovations) were Darabont and the two leads cast in the pivotal roles ofShawshank prison inmates Andy Dufresne and Red: Tim Robbins, who had just appearedin Robert Altman’s Short Cuts and The Player, and Morgan Freeman in his follow-up role toUnforgiven. Both actors reminisced about seizing the chance to act in the ﬁlm based on thestrength of what Robbins termed “the impenetrably perfect script.” After making a pair ofhigh-proﬁle horror ﬁlms, Darabont explained that he wrote Shawshank to avoid becomingtypecast in a genre already ﬁlled with maestros, though he had originally intended his ﬁrstfeature to be The Mist. The ﬁlm gathered an impressive ensemble of actors and technicians,many of whom were in attendance for the evening. (See the gallery below.)The majority of the ﬁlm’s production took place in Ohio, an area where the trio recalledbeing surrounded by good people, hail, heat and tornadoes; Robbins in particular wasthankful for the ﬁnal scene (one that was not in the original script, but suggested by CastleRock executives), which allowed them to wrap up the three-month shoot in St. Croix. Both actors also recalled the most diﬃcult scene they had to shoot, in which Robbinsemerges from a sewage pipe into an Ohio creek in the middle of “cow country .” (Fortunately ,showers were readily available.) Freeman and Darabont also remembered shooting anextensive panic attack scene near the end of the ﬁlm, which was cut from the ﬁnal versionafter test screening audiences indicated that they wanted Red to get to that pivotal tree rightaway .The ﬁlm didn't ﬁnd its target audience at ﬁrst, but positive word of mouth soon spread andmade Shawshank one of the most beloved ﬁlms of its era. Darabont, Robbins and Freemannoted that the ascendance of the ﬁlm’s reputation was gradual, but its fans around the globenow include residents of rural China and none other than the late Nelson Mandela. Some ofthe funniest mispronunciations of the title (found in the video gallery below) over the yearshave come from this remarkable diverse fanbase. Darabont, Robbins and Freeman, who individually have created impressive bodies of work,were delighted to reﬂect upon some of their lesser-known projects, and each named one ﬁlmthey wished more people would see. (Respectively they are The Majestic, Cradle Will Rock andBopha!) They also continue to keep the spirit of the Shawshank alive through their artistryand outside activities. Robbins still teaches acting to prison inmates, who are often surprisedto ﬁnd him in their company . “They love that movie,” Robbins said of Shawshank. “Guards,not so much!”VIDEOS & PHOTOSTHE SHAWSHANK REDEMPTION - 20TH ANNIVERSARY SCREENINGHOME/EVENTS/THE SHAWSHANK REDEMPTION
